---
title: "4.14 Searching Algorithms"
subtitle: "Data Collections"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Searching Algorithms

### Searching means locating a target efficiently in stored data

This topic frames searching as an <span class="term">algorithm family</span>.

::: {.tight-list}
- <span class="term">linear search</span> works on unsorted data
- <span class="term">binary search</span> requires sorted data
- the choice depends on the structure of the data and the allowed assumptions
:::

## Linear Search

### Check elements in order until the target is found or the data ends

```java
for (int j = 0; j < elements.length; j++)
{
    if (elements[j] == target)
    {
        return j;
    }
}
return -1;
```

Retained rules:

::: {.tight-list}
- it can begin from either end if the algorithm is written that way
- if no element matches, the search checks everything and returns `-1`
- with `String` values, use <span class="term">`equals`</span> instead of `==`
:::

## Linear Search with 2D Arrays

### Search each row, then each column position inside that row

Retained extension:

::: {.tight-list}
- a 2D search still uses linear search logic
- each row must be accessed before checking values in that row
- this is another case of reusing a 1D pattern inside nested loops
:::

## Binary Search

### Binary search only works when the data is already sorted

![Binary search shrinks the search space by halves](../../assets/4-data-collections/4-14-searching-algorithms/binary-search-small.gif){fig-align="center" width="58%"}

Core idea retained from the textbook:

::: {.tight-list}
- check the <span class="mark">middle</span> element first
- if the target is smaller, discard the right half
- if the target is larger, discard the left half
- each step removes about half of the remaining candidates
:::

## Binary Search Control Variables

### `left`, `right`, and `middle` define the active search range

```java
int left = 0;
int right = elements.length - 1;
while (left <= right)
{
    int middle = (left + right) / 2;
    ...
}
```

Students should retain:

::: {.tight-list}
- `left` starts at the first valid index
- `right` starts at the last valid index
- `middle` is recomputed each pass
- if the range disappears, the target was not found
:::

## Strings in Binary Search

### Ordered strings use `compareTo`, not numeric comparison operators

Retained AP rule:

::: {.tight-list}
- `target.compareTo(elements[middle]) < 0` means target belongs to the left side
- `> 0` means target belongs to the right side
- `== 0` in `compareTo` terms means the strings match
:::

## Runtime Comparison

### The main tradeoff is flexibility versus speed

| Algorithm | Works on unsorted data? | Worst-case idea |
|---|---|---|
| Linear search | Yes | Check all `n` elements |
| Binary search | No, must be sorted | Repeatedly cut the range in half |

Retained conclusion:

::: {.tight-list}
- linear search is more general
- binary search is typically more efficient on sorted data
- runtime comparisons often use the <span class="term">worst case</span>, especially “target not found”
:::

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- trace linear search on arrays and `ArrayList`s
- explain why `String` search uses `equals` or `compareTo`
- adapt linear search to a 2D array
- trace `left`, `right`, and `middle` through binary search
- compare worst-case steps for linear and binary search
- <span class="term">4.14.5 Coding Challenge: Search Runtimes</span>
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- explain when linear search is appropriate
- state the sorting precondition for binary search
- describe how binary search updates `left` and `right`
- distinguish `equals` from `compareTo` in string search contexts
- compare the worst-case behavior of linear and binary search
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
